copy.caption: Awkward on land, breathtaking in the water — the tuxedo is excellent camouflage.
copy.shortDescription: Penguins can't fly through the air but they absolutely fly through the sea — wings turned into flippers, reaching speeds of thirty-five kilometers per hour underwater, corkscrewing through kelp and launching off ledges to explode back onto ice. Their black-and-white pattern is camouflage: dark from above so sea predators can't see them against the depths, pale from below so nothing looking up sees them against the bright surface. Fun fact: Penguins' feathers are packed so tightly — up to a hundred per square centimeter — that water barely touches their skin even on the deepest dives.
